notes.observation: The paper states that statistical analyses were performed using Stata 18.0 and R 4.3.0, but does not provide access to raw data or code.

The paper reports primary and secondary outcomes, statistical methods, adverse events, and baseline characteristics. However, it does not report the full protocol or detailed data collection procedures beyond what is summarized.
The paper describes statistical methods and software used, but does not provide code or data access. The protocol is referenced but not included in the main text.

notes.observation: Conservatively assuming a mean difference of −34·33 cm² in abdominal VAT change between groups at 32 weeks, 43 participants per arm in a repeated measures design with two longitudinal measurements achieves >90% power. Sample size was inflated to 52 per arm to account for a conservative 20% loss-to-follow-up/missing data.

notes.observation: The primary outcome of interest, abdominal visceral AT, showed an estimated % change of −30·6% (β: −30·82 cm²; 95% CI: −50·13, −11·51).
quotes: abdominal visceral AT [β (95% CI); estimated % change: −30·82 cm 2 (−50·13, −11·51); −30·6%].

notes.observation: Benjamini-Hochberg procedure was used to adjust for multiple testing in all analyses; given the phase II nature of our trial, we selected a false discovery rate of 0·20 for correction of p values.
quotes: Benjamini-Hochberg procedure was used to adjust for multiple testing in all analyses; given the phase II nature of our trial, we selected a false discovery rate of 0·20 for correction of p values.

notes.observation: Missing data were random; thus, no adjustments were made for missingness in analyses.
quotes: Missing data were random; thus, no adjustments were made for missingness in analyses.

notes.observation: The worker returned an observation, but its quote did not match the normalized paper exactly.
The randomization sequence was held in a locked, secured location by the investigational pharmacy, and key personnel, staff, participants, and providers were masked to treatment assignment.
quotes: Key personnel, study staff, participants, and participants’ medical providers were masked to treatment assignment.

To preserve masking, investigational pharmacy assigned participants their treatment allocation at entry and dispensed all doses of semaglutide or matching placebo (saline) in identical-appearing, pre-filled syringes without any recognizable markings.

notes.observation: Eight participants in each group prematurely withdrew from the study prior to the 32-week primary endpoint. The study did not report on protocol deviations or reasons for withdrawal beyond premature discontinuation.

Primary outcomes were measured using DXA and non-contrast helical CT at weeks 0 and 32. Fat mass and density were assessed using standardized protocols, with a single radiologist masked to treatment assignment.
quotes: Body composition was assessed using whole-body dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry (DXA) and non-contrast helical computed tomography (CT) of chest and abdomen at weeks 0 and 32, as previously described.
Measurements were read by a single radiologist (KAG) masked to treatment assignment and clinical information.

notes.observation: Eight participants in each group withdrew prematurely, resulting in a 14.8% dropout rate. The study reported that missing data were random and no adjustments were made for missingness.
quotes: Eight participants in each group prematurely withdrew from the study prior to the 32-week primary endpoint.
Missing data were random; thus, no adjustments were made for missingness in analyses.

notes.observation: The paper makes strong claims about the efficacy of semaglutide in treating HIV-associated lipohypertrophy, particularly highlighting substantial reductions in abdominal visceral and subcutaneous fat, liver fat, and improvements in metabolic markers.
quotes: Notably, there were considerable reductions in all primary outcome measures, including TBF, trunk/abdominal fat (in both visceral and subcutaneous abdominal compartments), limb fat, and pericardial fat.
The estimated proportion of VAT loss was ~2·7x greater than SAT loss with an estimated reduction in VAT of ~30%.

notes.observation: The worker returned an observation, but its quote did not match the normalized paper exactly.
The paper uses causal language to attribute observed changes to semaglutide treatment, consistent with the randomized trial design.
quotes: Statistically significant treatment effects were seen for liver density (i.e., increased, suggestive of decreased liver fat).
The estimated proportion of VAT loss was ~2·7x greater than SAT loss with an estimated reduction in VAT of ~30%.

notes.observation: The paper acknowledges limitations that restrict generalizability, including small sample size, short duration, single-site enrollment, and exclusion of people with diabetes or severe lipoatrophy.
quotes: Roughly two-thirds of weight loss in our study was attributed to fat decreases. While fat loss carries beneficial effects for PWH considered overweight or obese, for some, there are potentially negative consequences to consider. Specifically, there are considerable numbers of PWH
We excluded people with severe lipoatrophy and observed limb fat losses nearly proportional to TBF.

notes.observation: The limitations discussed in the paper align with the study's design and findings, including sample size, duration, single-site enrollment, and methodological constraints.
quotes: Additional study limitations are worth mentioning. For example, we utilized density to measure fat quality which may fail to assess more subtle effects on adipocytes such as changes in macrophage infiltration or browning, as seen in other studies.
We also did not measure muscle strength or physical function, which would have aided in this investigation.

execution_warnings: 2 attempted judgments cited no permitted verified evidence and were excluded from scoring.

findings.explanation: No verified evidence was provided that describes the method of randomization (e.g., computer-generated sequence, random number table). The evidence list is empty for this item.
findings.title: Randomization Process: not assessed

findings.explanation: Allocation concealment is partially addressed: the investigational pharmacy held the randomization sequence in a secured location and dispensed identical-appearing syringes, which supports concealment. However, there is no explicit description of how the sequence was generated or whether the pharmacy was independent of the investigators, limiting full confidence in concealment.

paper_spans.quote: Key personnel, study staff, participants, and participants’ medical providers were masked to treatment assignment.

To preserve masking, investigational pharmacy assigned participants their treatment allocation at entry and dispensed all doses of semaglutide or matching placebo (saline) in identical-appearing, pre-filled syringes without any recognizable markings.

findings.title: Allocation Concealment: minor concern

findings.explanation: No verified evidence was provided regarding deviations from the intended intervention or protocol. The evidence list is empty for this item.
findings.title: Deviations: not assessed

findings.explanation: Outcome assessors were masked (a single radiologist masked to treatment), and standardized methods (DXA, CT) were used. However, the use of a single radiologist introduces potential bias due to lack of inter-rater reliability assessment, though masking mitigates some risk.

paper_spans.quote: Body composition was assessed using whole-body dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry (DXA) and non-contrast helical computed tomography (CT) of chest and abdomen at weeks 0 and 32, as previously described.
Measurements were read by a single radiologist (KAG) masked to treatment assignment and clinical information.

findings.title: Outcome Measurement: minor concern

findings.explanation: The study reports 8 dropouts per group (14.8% attrition) and states that missing data were random, with no adjustments made. While attrition is reported, the justification for assuming randomness without sensitivity analyses (e.g., MNAR models) introduces a minor concern for bias.

paper_spans.quote: Eight participants in each group prematurely withdrew from the study prior to the 32-week primary endpoint.
Missing data were random; thus, no adjustments were made for missingness in analyses.

findings.title: Attrition: minor concern

findings.explanation: The paper explicitly states that analyses followed intention-to-treat principles based on randomized assignment and included all available data, meeting the criterion for no concern.

paper_spans.quote: Analyses were performed using intention-to-treat principles based on randomised treatment assignment including all available data.

findings.title: Intention To Treat: no concern

findings.explanation: The study reports a power calculation justifying the sample size (>90% power to detect a mean difference in VAT change), with inflation to account for 20% loss-to-follow-up, indicating adequate planning for statistical power.

paper_spans.quote: Conservatively assuming a mean difference of −34·33 cm 2 in abdominal VAT change between groups at 32 weeks, 43 participants per arm in a repeated measures design with two longitudinal measurements achieves >90% power. Sample size was inflated to 52 per arm to account for a conservative 20% loss-to-follow-up/missing data.

findings.title: Power: no concern

findings.explanation: The paper reports effect sizes with 95% confidence intervals (e.g., β = −30.82 cm², 95% CI: −50.13, −11.51), fulfilling the requirement for effect size and precision reporting.

paper_spans.quote: abdominal visceral AT [β (95% CI); estimated % change: −30·82 cm 2 (−50·13, −11·51); −30·6%].

findings.title: Effect Size Interval: no concern

findings.explanation: The Benjamini-Hochberg procedure was used to adjust for multiple testing with a false discovery rate of 0.20, which is appropriate for a phase II trial and addresses multiplicity concerns.

paper_spans.quote: Benjamini-Hochberg procedure was used to adjust for multiple testing in all analyses; given the phase II nature of our trial, we selected a false discovery rate of 0·20 for correction of p values.

findings.title: Multiplicity: no concern

findings.explanation: The paper states that missing data were random and no adjustments were made. However, without reporting the method used to assess randomness (e.g., Little’s test) or conducting sensitivity analyses, there is a minor concern about handling of missing data.

paper_spans.quote: Missing data were random; thus, no adjustments were made for missingness in analyses.

findings.title: Missing Data: minor concern

findings.explanation: The paper makes strong claims about semaglutide’s efficacy, supported by statistically significant and clinically meaningful results across multiple fat compartments, consistent with the reported findings.

paper_spans.quote: Notably, there were considerable reductions in all primary outcome measures, including TBF, trunk/abdominal fat (in both visceral and subcutaneous abdominal compartments), limb fat, and pericardial fat.
The estimated proportion of VAT loss was ~2·7x greater than SAT loss with an estimated reduction in VAT of ~30%.

findings.title: Claim Strength: no concern

findings.explanation: No verified evidence was provided to evaluate whether the results directly support the claims made. The evidence list is empty for this item.
findings.title: Results Support: not assessed

findings.explanation: The paper uses causal language (e.g., 'treatment effects') appropriately, which is justified by the randomized controlled trial design, supporting valid causal interpretation.

paper_spans.quote: Statistically significant treatment effects were seen for liver density (i.e., increased, suggestive of decreased liver fat).
The estimated proportion of VAT loss was ~2·7x greater than SAT loss with an estimated reduction in VAT of ~30%.

findings.title: Causal Language: no concern

findings.explanation: The paper acknowledges limitations to generalizability (small sample, single site, exclusion of diabetes and severe lipoatrophy), but still makes broad claims about effects in people with HIV, introducing minor concern about overgeneralization.

paper_spans.quote: Roughly two-thirds of weight loss in our study was attributed to fat decreases. While fat loss carries beneficial effects for PWH considered overweight or obese, for some, there are potentially negative consequences to consider. Specifically, there are considerable numbers of PWH with lipoatrophy, most often apparent in their faces and limbs.
We excluded people with severe lipoatrophy and observed limb fat losses nearly proportional to TBF.

findings.title: Generalization: minor concern

findings.explanation: The limitations discussed (e.g., use of density for fat quality, lack of muscle function measures) are directly relevant to the study’s methods and findings, showing strong alignment.

paper_spans.quote: Additional study limitations are worth mentioning. For example, we utilized density to measure fat quality which may fail to assess more subtle effects on adipocytes such as changes in macrophage infiltration or browning, as seen in other studies.
We also did not measure muscle strength or physical function, which would have aided in this investigation.

findings.title: Limitations Alignment: no concern

identity.title: Once-weekly semaglutide in people with HIV-associated lipohypertrophy: a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase 2b single-centre clinical trial

limitations: The Review score summarizes assessed methodology items; it is not the probability that the conclusions are true.
No cited-paper full texts were retrieved or analyzed.
English is validated first; other languages are experimental.
Supplementary files were linked by the article, but their file contents were not parsed.
2 attempted judgments cited no permitted verified evidence and were excluded from scoring.
summary: Generalization: minor concern. The paper acknowledges limitations to generalizability (small sample, single site, exclusion of diabetes and severe lipoatrophy), but still makes broad claims about effects in people with HIV, introducing minor concern about overgeneralization.

Allocation Concealment: minor concern. Allocation concealment is partially addressed: the investigational pharmacy held the randomization sequence in a secured location and dispensed identical-appearing syringes, which supports concealment. However, there is no explicit description of how the sequence was generated or

Attrition: minor concern. The study reports 8 dropouts per group (14.8% attrition) and states that missing data were random, with no adjustments made. While attrition is reported, the justification for assuming randomness without sensitivity analyses (e.g., MNAR models) introduces a minor concern for bias

Outcome Measurement: minor concern. Outcome assessors were masked (a single radiologist masked to treatment), and standardized methods (DXA, CT) were used. However, the use of a single radiologist introduces potential bias due to lack of inter-rater reliability assessment, though masking mitigates some risk.
The final review assessed 17 of 24 methodology items.
